item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept is interesting, the writing is good, but still I don't feel nothing for this as it is.

In the end, the article is very clever to give the hints as to what the skip really does, but does nothing with it. It isn't harmful (though probably it is to the Foundation's pockets), it isn't dangerous, it doesn't tell a story, it does nothing whatsoever aside being a puzzle for the reader.

Maybe a recovery log could solve this. Or even a log of experiments that had actual relevance, even if not finding nothing about the thing. Until now the only consequence was the death of a single lab rat.

On my final note, their failure at the end didn't strike me so well. Maybe it could be better to say that the anti-meme guys simply told them to lock the thing and not bother any longer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it would be better if you take the "DHMO" reference, specifically, back out. When I came across it all of a sudden I started feeling that I was reading a shaggy dog story all along, oh no, this guy's wasting my time. The other names for the substance were less of a trigger for me, if that helps.

It's actually an interesting antimemetic SCP, in my opinion, because it's so ordinary. The farther you get from DHMO associations the better it will be.

Here's an idea: Replace all of the Oxidane, DHMO and hydroxylic acid references entirely and just use the common name where they appear (all toward the end). The researchers still can't tell the difference!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On a scale of 1 to even, I just can't. Can we grow past the DHMO crap please? I don't think an SCP that is literally just that stupid joke really works, even as a cognitohazard. Maybe as a -J. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anti-memetic water? Sounds like something that should be "accidentally" exposed to SCP-402.

Though for all I know, this could be written from the perspective of researchers treated to remove the deadly effects of the SCP that's an invisible second moon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I recognized DHMO immediately, but still had to come to the comments for the aha! moment. It helps that DHMO is only one of the listed names, none of which are repeated- I recognized this one but not the others, and thus wound up back in the same territory as the article, where I believed it was constantly turning into other things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Came here in search for new antimemetic articles. Discovered a rehash of the old DHMO joke. Given how good all the previous antimemetic articles were, this one is somewhat disappointing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, and until the second reading I was thinking like 'oh DMSO, that's gotta be toxic huh?'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think there's anything wrong with "dihydrogen monoxide" from a technical standpoint.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A quick criticism might be that DHMO/"dihydrogen monoxide" is a relatively faux-scientific term in most contexts (as far as I know), and the inclusion of it in this article as a scientific alternative for water, in my opinion as a reader, makes for a bit of a break in the immersion of the article. However, I will say that the article as a whole is very clever, and I didn't even catch it until I went to the comment section (though I was so close!).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for helping out those of us who are slow on the uptake. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this could be written a little better, but you probably couldn't add too much more detail without spoiling it. I gave it an upvote because I like the simpler articles and I like stuff that's not overtly dangerous.

I'm kind of curious as to how it came into being, though. If it came from a larger source that was identifiable, then the article would probably be about the source instead…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this one works well as a mainlister exactly because it takes a joke idea and makes it serious. In-universe, everybody is legitimately freaking out over SCP-2938 because they are unable to perceive what it is, but can still see its "harmful" effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd feel a lot more comfortable with this as a -J.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And from what I've read, the Foundation has two mnestic divisions, neither of which know of each other.

"Mnestics" are drugs, you probably mean "antimeme divisions".

Also, what kind of idiot would write about two divisions of conflicting mutually invisible antimeme guys? :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And from what I've read, the Foundation has two mnestic divisions, neither of which know of each other. However, these divisions are both highly secretive, and at times, seem to operate as a sort of 'Foundation within the Foundation'.

Even if they noticed it, having the documentation describe 2938 as it is keeps it contained. Why risk exposing themselves to the rest of the Foundation if the SCP is contained?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had to read it twice and then read the comments to figure out that this was water and now I feel sort of stupid. Although the idea of antimemetic water is really interesting, +1.

item.title: Re: Should it be tagged "antimemetic"?
item.content:encoded: I think you're right, the tag doesn't really apply.

item.title: Should it be tagged "antimemetic"?
item.content:encoded: Considering that the real anomaly is that it cannot be perceived as water (suppressing the comprehension of the concept), would that count as anti-memetic?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My sentiment exactly, Canaryfarmer!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reads like it's just a tongue-in-cheek DHMO hoax reference and I don't know how to feel about that, since I've personally had to explain that crap to terrified morons on more than one occasion, and it stems from the same kind of stupidity that's making the anti-vaxxer movement popular.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't forget about the DHMO! (link is safe and free of spoilers)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And I do distinctly remember that the first time I read the skip, I pictured it (by default!) as more of a gel

I did the same thing—I mentally established something clear and goopy straight out of the Crystal Pepsi Gravy SNL commercial, which definitely pushed me away from realizing it was YOU KNOW.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mass/weight instead of volume: yeah, but Drewbear, my love, isn't this a little tiny teeny weeny bit cheating? ;>

the cognitohazard makes the researchers unable to identify that they can use liquid measurements

Okay. I accept this. Reluctantly, but I do. But it makes it VERY difficult for the average reader to understand the skip.

As for the 1.7 kg thing, I like putting the numbers 17 & 37 into my articles.

I didn't mean the number you picked, I meant that particular amount of █████ that is anomalous, as opposed to the rest of it everywhere that is not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a niggling feeling that it is certainly possible to design an expirement that will indicate the cognitohazard. Mnestics? Blind piecemeal analysis? Some clever combination of blinds and controls?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, they did notice evidence of cognitohazards when the evidence was initially reviewed. But when they tried to compensate & re-test, the effect encompassed them and made them discount there being a cognitohazard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
TBlackburn pretty much has it for why I used mass/weight instead of volume.

As for the 1.7 kg thing, I like putting the numbers 17 & 37 into my articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was my intent, yeah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It probably has to do with the fact that we can not identify basic items that make [spoiler] what it is. If my chemistry classes serve me right, 1 liter of [spoiler] is supposed to weigh 1 kilo of it, too.

So in this case the amounts of grams and millilitres are interchangeable to indicate the absolute amount of [spoiler] used in the testing. And because this is an identifying feature of [spoiler] the cogitohazard makes the researchers unable to identify that they can use liquid measurements rather than solid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The main merit of this skip is that it makes you think. Hard. ;>

I'm still not bought on it. Getting there, but not yet.

+ show block
– hide block

Both in universe for tone, and out of universe for … not cheating! wouldn't it be a good idea to mention in the chart of test logs, first column:
5 g liquid sample etc., all the way down? (or 5 ml liquid sample)
Because right now, you describe it as a "clear colourless substance…" but give no precision about the state (solid/liquid/gaseous) of the substance for tests, which comes across as not very scientific, IMO. And I do distinctly remember that the first time I read the skip, I pictured it (by default!) as more of a gel - picturing it as a liquid would have helped me grok much sooner.
(I also remember that at the 0° degree thing, I thought 'Huh. This sounds familiar…'!)
And, while we're at it, why 1,7 kg of the substance instead of 1,7 litres, since the main natural state of █████ is a liquid, and we usually measure quantities of liquid in litres?
Or is this part of the cognitohazard; not being able to identify the state of the substance?
Speaking of which: why mention the cognitohazard at the end and then negate it immediately?

Also: what makes this particular 1,7 kg special? Don't we need a teeny weeny tad of history here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's too short for it's own good, and the addendum seems like an almost nonsensical inclusion that doesn't gel with the rest of this. I liked the tests, it almost reminds me of the physics chamber, but it's not doing enough for me right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't that be evidence of a cognitohazard to a person who wasn't directly exposed? Or failing that wouldn't automated checking (possibly by AIAD) find the obvious logical error in that statement?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
head-asplodey.

Seriously though, given this specific scenario, they'd probably insist that the oxidanes were different, but not be able to explain why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So when asked about the instance of "oxidane" in this document, they will describe it as a inorganic compound of two parts hydrogen to one part oxygen and so on? If asked if that is the same as [spoiler] they will say no? But if asked again if oxidane is the same as [spoiler] they will say yes? What happens if you ask them if this oxidane is the same as that oxidane?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [spoiler]. But they wouldn't be able to connect that to 2938, even if it's specifically pointed out to them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At least, I thought it was something like SCP-009… but now, I guess it is rather witty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So if someone who is exposed to SCP-2938 is asked to define "oxidane" what do they say?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Extremely simplistic, but seeing as how that's the point, I can't really complain.

I think that after reading the comments and understanding the gist behind this thing, I'm gonna tentatively upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Had to read the comments to get it. Still wondering why the k-class scenario tag is relevant. DHMO is a pretty silly way to go, honestly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The effect is currently limited to SCP-2938 only, so people are free to call unaffected/untainted [spoiler] by its common name. Propogation occurs with contact/admixture with SCP-2938, including materials that have already been converted to 2938. The rest of the world's [spoiler] hasn't been affected yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Where does the inability to call [spoiler] by its common name come in then? Do people who have been exposed start asking for cold glasses of formanol with some oxidane crystals? Does no one notice? Or if propagates by this, why hasn't this spread?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
^ Apparently, identifying that SCP-2938 is [spoiler] triggers the effect, even via indirect knowledge. They would not be aware of this however, because it is not an unexpected result that the substance cannot be identified.
Mini-philosophy lesson.
There are five orders of ignorance:
0th: Lack of Ignorance: I completely comprehend X.
1st: Order Ignorance: I don't know these specific aspects of X
2nd Order Ignorance:I know there are things I don't know about X, but I don't know what they are
3rd Order Ignorance: I know that I don't know whether I know everything about X or not.
4th Order Ignorance: I am unaware that I don't know everything about X

The cogitohazard seems to trigger at 2nd order, and prevent anyone from progressing beyond 2nd order. They know there is something they don't know, and have correctly identified that there is a cogitohazard that prevents them from moving beyond that, but the nature of the cogitohazard prevents them from identifying SCP-2938 beyond these traits. You can establish tests to identify SCP-2938, but you cannot comprehend the results. Even blind shotgunning attempts with standard testing batteries are foiled by the fact that knowing enough to identify SCP-2938 as [spoiler] apparently triggers the cogitohazard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But someone who has never been exposed to SCP-2938, like logically the person responsible for reviewing SCP reports, is going to identify this as [spoiler] and design the tests. It doesn't make any sense for tests for possible cognitive hazards to be designed by persons exposed to said hazards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I see what's going on. You appear to be unconsciously assuming that, because you know that SCP-2938 is actually [spoiler], that the Foundation would know that they need to test it against that. But they don't. They wouldn't choose [spoiler] as a control group because, as far as they can tell, SCP-2938's properties don't match any known substance. You can't have a control group if you don't know what the alternative is/what you're controlling for.

Edit: They'd be equally as likely to test it against mercury or powdered gelatin or Alka-Seltzer as [spoiler] and would find the same amount of "hey, this doesn't make sense". It's like you're looking at two sheets of blue paper and despite being identical, there's something about one of them that makes you not be able to connect that it's blue or that it's paper or even that it's made out of wood pulp, to the fact that it's a blue piece of paper identical to the other one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So how did blind controls fail? If the researchers are given just [spoiler] as an unknown and told to analyze it they say "This is just [spoiler]. Hey are we the control group?" - right? How does that fail?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The original mass of SCP-2938 has to contaminate [spoiler] first and hasn't yet done so. Put it into [appropriate large mass of spoiler] and it'd convert it all in a matter of moments, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it's strong enough that attempts to compensate for it don't work.

How hasn't this affected everyone then if it also affects blind controls? Wouldn't there be global panic as people perceive [spoiler] as a foreign substance with inexplicable properties?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because the cognitohazard effect makes it literally impossible for them to recognize that this is [spoiler]. And while it's not quite subtle enough (which is where the data irregularities came from), it's strong enough that attempts to compensate for it don't work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's actually more confusing to me. If I were to replicate these tests with a known control of [spoiler], I would be able to replicate these results exactly. How did it possibly fail?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point about the Class. If they knew/could acknowledge its real effect, it'd be Euclid because they can't adequately compensate for/contain it. But because they don't, they'd call it Safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the addition of the DHMO really makes it more clear what the "ah-ha" is. Good edit.

That being said, while this might still fly over some peoples' heads, I think it's a clever SCiP and is thought-provoking enough to merit attention. All the smaller chemistry jokes are good to those who get them, and the whole of the article is enough to leave the uninformed perplexed with enough of a sense of dread.

+1

edit: is it Euclid because of its properties? Because I think the nature of its containment is enough for a "Safe."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Added a bit about that, to show that they did notice, tried to compensate, and failed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still like formanol, the base-level alcohol. The generalized equation is CnH2n+1OH, and in this case, n=0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually thought about using DHMO, but decided against it as being a little too on-the-nose. I might need to revisit that.

EDIT: On reflection, I added DHMO after all, as well as expanding a bit on the contagious nature of the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's the dreaded dihydrogen monoxide, which is one of my favorite gags, but somehow I didn't pick up on it. Neutral because it could be I just didn't read carefully enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, the actual effect here isn't really explored at all. What happens when other Foundation researchers review this without exposure? Shouldn't they be like, "What is going on here? Isn't this just [spoiler]?" Then the real research into this effect would start.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Right.
So thingy is named oxidane by IUPAC and frozen thingy is an acid crystal.
I thought I was on Earth right now, but I must have taken a wrong turn on the way…! ;-D

So, to sum up:
- Drewbear's idea for a skip may well be the most splendiferous idea ever put forth for a skip;
- the Devil is in the details, and in the writing!

Thank you for the explanations, Sirpudding & WJS!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay. So you have to be a graduate in Chemistry to understand this skip…

I am, to my eternal shame, only a High School graduate (and that but barely).

+ show block
– hide block

What's this got to do with H2O?

Everything.

If you pour water into a different container you will have briefly two distinct masses of water, which will combine (you can see this in time lapse photos of pouring; or just pour a little bit of water into your glass and then add some more later).

At 50° water is in a liquid state, and it contains water (which under IUPAC conventions would be named oxidane).

At 0° water is a solid (which we call "ice" in English) that is a crystal made of water (which if you name it as an acid is hydroxylic acid).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay. So you have to be a graduate in Chemistry to understand this skip…

Mmmh.

Plus:

+ show block
– hide block

SCP-2938 initially separated in 2 distinct masses, which then operated according to an unknown force to rejoin.
At 50ºC, converted into a liquid suspension containing large amounts of oxidane.
At 0ºC, converted into a crystalline solid composed of hydroxylic acid. No further changes noted.

What's this got to do with H2O?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not especially good at dissolving non-polar solutes compared to non-polar solvents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Incidentally, SCP-2938 is one of the most potent solvents known to man, able to cut through almost any ionic bond. It's no wonder that when exposed to a living organism, the organism quickly adapts for full use of SCP-2938. It's that efficient of a carrier of molecules.
